data.title: Best Dividend Kings: January 2026
data.description:
2025 Review

2025 will be a year to forget for the Dividend Kings as, on average, they only mustered a 4.91% total return. While earning a positive return by being invested in some of the most tenured dividend growers is never a bad thing, underperforming the broad equity market is seldom an easy thing to swallow. The SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) finished last year with a total return of 17.72%, far outpacing the average Dividend King.

Despite the lackluster performance last year, 14 Dividend Kings still managed to deliver superior total returns relative to SPY.

Best Dividend Kings in 2025

As of year-end 2025, nineteen Dividend Kings have achieved double-digit total returns, but only fourteen outperformed SPY. These were the best-performing Kings:

  1. Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) +47.49%
  2. Nucor Corporation (NUE) +42.05%
  3. Parker-Hannifin (PH) +39.54%
  4. National Fuel Gas (NFG) +35.32%
  5. Canadian Utilities Limited (CDUAF) +35.10%
  6. AbbVie (ABBV) +33.13%
  7. Fortis (FTS) +29.62%
  8. The Gorman-Rupp Company (GRC) +28.26%
  9. Walmart (WMT) +24.49%
  10. Black Hills Corp. (BKH) +23.90%
  11. Northwest Natural Holding Company (NWN) +23.69%
  12. Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) +18.30%
  13. Altria Group (MO) +18.20%
  14. Tootsie Roll (TR) +17.87%

Of the remaining 42 Dividend Kings, 5 have a double-digit positive gain, 8 have single-digit gains, and 29 are in the red. The worst-performing Dividend King last year was Stepan Company (SCL) with a loss of 24.57%. Not too far from the bottom was Target (TGT) with a loss of 24.46%. In total, 16 Dividend Kings posted double-digit losses.

2026 has kicked off very well for the Dividend Kings, as they are, on average, up 4.66% through January 23rd, compared to a gain of 1.07% for SPY.

The five best-performing Dividend Kings in January are:

  1. Stepan Company +18.18%
  2. Archer Daniel Midland +17.43%
  3. Lowe's Companies (LOW) +14.75%
  4. MSA Safety (MSA) +13.27%
  5. Nordson (NDSN) +12.86%

Dividend Growth

The average dividend growth rate for all of the Dividend Kings in 2025 was 5.81%. Several increases have already been announced and documented for 2026. Additionally, some of the Kings will organically pay a higher dividend amount in calendar year 2026, relative to 2025, just due to the timing of their increases last year. In total, 45 out of the 57 Kings already locked in another year of dividend growth in 2026, most without yet announcing a dividend increase.

As of today, the average dividend growth rate for all of the Dividend Kings in 2026 stands at 2.07%.

With not much to cover in the current year thus far, perhaps I can recap the 6 best dividend growth rates from the prior year.

RLI Corp.

Increased its dividend payout from $0.45 to $0.63, or by 40.31%

ABM Industries

Increased its dividend payout from $0.90 to $1.06, or by 17.78%

Walmart

Increased its dividend payout from $0.81 to $0.94, or by 15.69%

Automatic Data Processing

Increased its dividend payout from $5.60 to $6.32, or by 12.86%

Parker-Hannifin

Increased its dividend payout from $6.37 to $7.03, or by 10.36%

Grainger

Increased its dividend payout from $8.01 to $8.83, or by 10.24%

Earnings Projections

Since last month's update, analyst 3-5 year earnings growth projections have trended lower by -0.24%, on average for all the Dividend Kings.

11 Dividend Kings are currently projected to grow earnings by double digits over the next 3-5 years.

  1. AbbVie +16.26%
  2. Abbott Laboratories +10.07%
  3. Dover Corporation +11.67%
  4. H.B. Fuller (FUL) +10.87%
  5. Gorman Rupp Company +12.92%
  6. Nucor Corporation +15.47%
  7. Northwest Natural Holdings +11.22%
  8. Parker Hannifin +10.13%
  9. Stepan Company +28.24%
  10. S&P Global (SPGI) +12.52%
  11. Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) +14.52%

New Dividend King

With the new year, we also have one new Dividend King joining this coveted dividend growth list; that King is Pentair (PNR). Pentair has already increased its dividend in 2026 from $0.25 per share to $0.27 per share, or by 8%. The stock currently appears to be potentially 28% overvalued based on Dividend Yield Theory and sports a 6.68% future return estimate.

Promising Kings in 2026

In last month's update, as of December 25th, I identified 22 Dividend Kings who appeared to be both potentially undervalued and had a strong long-term return estimate. My article was published on December 28th, and from December 29th to January 23rd, these 22 Kings had an average total return of +3.45%, compared to +3.62% for all 57 Dividend Kings and +0.20% for SPY. Slightly worse than all of the Dividend Kings and far better than SPY.

While this short-term performance is interesting to review, these Kings are chosen with a long-term mindset as part of the expected future return is based on reversion back to the mean.

As of January 23rd, 19 Dividend Kings appear to be both potentially undervalued and have a long-term annualized expected rate of return of at least 10%. Here they are:

Dividend Yield Theory & Expected Rate of Return

I utilize Dividend Yield Theory as my primary valuation model for this analysis. Here is a brief explanation of how it works as well as my expected rate of return.

Dividend yield theory compares the forward dividend yield to the trailing 5-year average dividend yield to measure undervaluation. The expected long-term annualized rate of return is measured as follows:

Expected ROR = 3-5 year analyst earnings growth forecast + forward dividend yield + 5-year annualized return for fair value based on dividend yield theory

data.title: FIGS: Overvaluation Leads To A Sell Rating
data.description:
One company that I have been aware of for quite some time now but have never really dug deep into is FIGS (FIGS). Coming into 2026, I told myself that I wanted to spend time analyzing more companies than I have in the past. And as an interesting player in its space, it certainly is deserving of a deep dive. What I found when analyzing the company is that, even though revenue has grown nicely, profitability has been quite mixed. The good news is that the 2025 fiscal year was shaping up well, and the stock has a fortress balance sheet. Its overall market presence is growing, and I do think that outweighs the volatility on the bottom line. Unfortunately, there is one big downside, and this is the fact that shares are very expensive. In fact, they are so expensive that I think investors would be wise to take a cautious approach here. That's why, even though it pains me to do so, I believe that initiating this prospect with a ‘sell’ rating is the right choice.

Not a Great Fit

The management team at FIGS describes the company as a direct-to-consumer health care apparel and lifestyle brand. The company caters to healthcare professionals through the scrub-aware and non-scrub-aware that it provides. Examples here include outerwear, underscrubs, footwear, compression socks, lab coats, loungewear, and other apparel. The company designs these in-house for the most part and then utilizes third-party suppliers and producers for the purpose of delivering said items. These are then sold through its digital platform, which involves not only a website but also its mobile app. It also has its own B2B business, and it operates physical retail stores called Community Hubs.

Management views this as an attractive opportunity for long-term growth. Even though a lot of the company's efforts have been dedicated to the US market, it pointed out accurately in its latest investor presentation that over 80% of global healthcare professionals live and work outside of the US. However, they account for only 16% of the firm's revenue at this time. In fact, the business has taken a very active role in international expansion. This has included 12 locations opened in the third quarter of 2025 in Latin America and 11 that were planned to be opened between the Middle East and Africa in the final quarter of last year. In fact, the firm forecasted that, by the end of 2025, it would have sales coming from nearly 60 different countries. That's almost double the 33 countries that it had exposure to back in 2024.

There does seem to be a lot of interest in what the company provides. In fact, according to data collected by management, over 60% of people who are not its customers but who do work in the healthcare space want to try and feel a product before they purchase it. That's why this physical retail strategy that the company champions makes a lot of sense conceptually. Of course, this is a rather small piece of the pie right now, with the firm only operating two such locations as of early 2025. However, the company targeted three additional Community Hubs to open in the final quarter of last year. These would have been New York City, Houston, and Chicago. The other two that were in operation were located in Los Angeles and Philadelphia. So management is clearly targeting top-tier markets with high population density and large employment in the healthcare sector.

Over the last few years, management has achieved attractive growth. Between 2022 and 2024, revenue expanded from $505.8 million to $555.6 million. As revenue grows, you would expect profitability to increase also. But this has not always been the case. After seeing net income rise from $21.2 million in 2022 to $22.6 million in 2023, there was a drop to just $2.7 million in 2024. This decline was driven by a jump in operating costs, primarily selling expenses that surged from 22.9% of sales to 25.5%. According to the company, this was driven mostly by higher fulfillment expenses as the company transitioned its fulfillment operations to a new center and also because of higher shipping costs caused by lower average order value. But I will get to that in just a moment. Cash flows, as the aforementioned chart illustrates, have also been rather lumpy.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year, the picture was looking up more than it did in previous years. Revenue of $429.2 million in the first nine months ended up representing a 6.3% increase over the $403.7 million the company reported a year earlier. This growth brings us back to a very important key metric for the company. This would be the number of active customers that it has. In the image below, you can see the total number of active customers, as well as the net revenue per active customer. From 2022 through 2024, the active customer count went from 2.29 million to 2.67 million. The 2.78 million reported for the third quarter of 2025 exceeded the 2.67 million reported the previous year.

The increase in the number of Community Hubs, which management promised in their latest earnings call to accelerate moving forward, certainly will play some role here moving forward. But up to this point, it seems as though this expansion was driven largely by what management described as a ‘promotional reset.’ Management described repeat frequency rising, as well as customers that were previously inactive reactivating. In fact, reactivations were up significantly according to management. Some of this can be chalked up to the fact that, after the COVID-19 pandemic, there was an easing in demand for the health care sector. However, it appears as though this overhang has itself eased. And what we are seeing now is a return to a more normal environment. When combined with strong promotional activity, it makes sense that the firm would see some strength here. The aforementioned image also highlights a return to growth when it comes to net revenue per active customer. And as the image below illustrates, this was driven largely by average order value increasing substantially from $108 to $114.

The rise in revenue did bring with it this time higher profitability. Net income went from only $0.8 million to $15.7 million year over year. We did see operating cash flow plummet from $50.7 million to $0.2 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $40.2 million to $46.9 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $30.7 million to $47.8 million. When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year as a whole, management made the case that revenue would rise by about 7%. And with that, the company would achieve an EBITDA margin of around 10.3%. If these figures do come to fruition, this would translate to EBITDA of $61.2 million, which would be up nicely from the $51.8 million reported for 2024. And if we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, adjusted operating cash flow would come in at around $71.8 million.

With these estimates, it's easy to see how the stock is valued. But unfortunately, shares are very expensive. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean. These are the kinds of multiples you expect from a high-growth technology play. However, FIGS is neither high growth nor overlay technology-centric. Relative to other similar firms, shares are a bit pricey also. In that chart, I compared the company to five other similar firms. Three of the five ended up being cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. But when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, our candidate was the most expensive of the group.

It is worth noting that one really positive thing about the company is that it has what I would consider to be a fortress balance sheet. Management has been principled enough to stay away from debt entirely. And on top of having no debt on its books, the company has cash and cash equivalents of $241.5 million. That is pretty sizable for a company with a market capitalization of only $2.03 billion. And it certainly provides some financial flexibility should times get tough or should management decide to use this for fuel for additional growth.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I would argue that while FIGS is most certainly not an awful company, it is a very expensive one. It has a unique business model, and I suspect that revenue will continue growing in the long run. I don't particularly like how volatile financial performance has been, but I do appreciate the recent improvements in profitability. Even so, the stock is incredibly expensive, even accounting for the strong balance sheet. And that's why I think that rating it a soft ‘sell’ makes sense here.

data.title: Ambarella: Powering Physical AI Beyond The Data Center
data.description:
Overview

With the increase in demand for AI, many aspects of what people refer to as "AI work" are being transitioned to edge devices, including cameras, self-driving vehicles, drones, and industrial assets and systems. Edge devices must perceive and act in real time and thus can't always rely on the cloud to make those imperative decisions. Here, the parallel opportunity created for smaller, niche semiconductor manufacturers is very much evidenced by Ambarella (AMBA).

Ambarella is an American semiconductor company that designs AI & computer vision processors used to make smart cameras and sensor systems work better. By designing a system-on-chip (SoC), highly integrated processors are able to combine signal processing, video stream encoding/decoding, and on-device AI inference into one platform to meet all the demands of edge devices to help cameras and sensors understand what they see in real time. Thus, Ambarella improves the performance of edge devices that are physically constrained by battery life, heat emissions, bandwidth availability, privacy regulations, and latency. In a nutshell, Ambarella is providing both the capture and real-time understanding of video at the edge.

Strong Revenue Growth

As the edge AI pivot continues, Ambarella's financial performance trajectory reflects that same positive direction. In the fiscal Q3 2026 release (quarter ending October 31, 2025), Ambarella posted record revenue of $108.5M (+31.2% compared to the same quarter a year ago). Non-GAAP gross margins stood at 60.9%, and it reported a non-GAAP EPS of $0.27.

In addition, total cash, cash equivalents, and marketable debt securities were reported at $295.3 million. For the full fiscal year, revenue is expected to be between 36% and 38%. In Q4, it projects revenues to be in the range of $97 million to $103 million. The results are expected to be released on February 24th after market close, with an EPS GAAP Estimate of -$0.51 and a revenue estimate of $100.18M.

In the meantime, Ambarella made its CV7 product announcement at CES on January 5, 2026. This is a 4-nanometer (nm) edge-AI vision SoC, or the combination of simultaneous multi-stream video plus advanced on-device AI, all powered by a very low-power footprint. The news release announced the SoC capability of multi-stream processing up to 8Kp60.

Significant Market Opportunities

Targeted applications of SoC are security cameras, robotics/drones, industrial applications, and multi-stream applications associated with automotive. The launch of the CV7 indicates a good performance per watt increase from this product and draws 20% less power than its last-generation equivalent.

Ambarella is necessary to address the needs of customers in edge-AI applications for "physical world" computing. Edge customers are focused on maximizing productivity in security cameras, fleet systems, robots, and vehicles that need to generate real-time actionable metadata (detection, classification, and events) without transmitting large quantities of high-resolution video to the cloud. The cloud is slow and costly for always-on perception. The edge offers a faster, cost-effective, and more private alternative for inference. That is the reason why demand for edge-AI solutions continues to grow, even when most of the market is enamored with purchases of data center GPUs. In terms of total addressable market by application area, the video surveillance market is one of the largest market opportunities, with the most relevant growth area coming from AI-empowered video security solutions.

According to Grand View Research, the market size for AI in video surveillance will grow from $7 billion in 2024 to an estimated $29 billion by 2030. Further, it estimates the global video surveillance market to grow from $73 billion in 2024 to $147 billion by 2030. This shows that even a minor "AI attach" to an existing large installed base can result in significant demand. Specifically, the CV7's ability to support multiple streams and sensors is aligned with the direction that high-end security systems are moving towards: multi-imager cameras and richer analytics on the device itself.

In fleet video telematics and driver monitoring, fleets implement real-time alerts, coaching, and incident triage, making edge AI increasingly important. Global Market Insights forecasts the video telematics market to grow from $1.7 billion in 2024 to $8.7 billion by 2034. That growth supports the need for more compute at the endpoint, where there are more cameras, more types of modelling (e.g., distraction and collision risk), and more local processing to manage bandwidth and cloud costs.

In robotics and industrial automation, perception is the enabling layer for both autonomy and machine vision. Here, Markets and Markets is projecting that the robotic vision market will reach $3.3 billion in 2025 and grow to $5 billion by 2030. While these markets may not fit into the HyperScaler TAM bracket, these areas are especially relevant to Ambarella, as they are all inherently edge native, meaning that they have low latency, highly reliable, and have very tight power budgets, which is precisely where performance per watt needs to be maximized.

Finally, in drones, autonomy is just as important as imaging, which means AI inference needs to occur on-device. This is arguably the biggest market opportunity for Ambarella, given the significant growth of drones for both commercial and military use cases. Here, the estimated global drone market is estimated to be worth $73B in 2024 and could more than double to $163B by 2030.

Finally, with regard to automotive, the opportunity for Ambarella is a longer cycle based on the increase in sensor and perception computation per vehicle that continues to grow. Here, automotive could be a $32B opportunity by 2030. Other research suggests more aggressive growth with an expectation of a $102B market size for automotive ADAS sensors, suggesting a CAGR of 33% from 2023 to 2028. In either case, more sensors mean there are more perception computing requirements and thus an increased demand for silicon platforms capable of efficiently processing multiple data streams.

The Developer Zone

As part of Ambarella's edge-AI strategy, the Developer Zone (DevZone) is an often overlooked and critical component of building a strong ecosystem. Thus, Ambarella recently announced DevZone, a new web portal that will provide customers with a simpler way to develop and deploy edge AI applications utilizing Ambarella's SoCs via optimized models and tools for developing Edge AI applications with Cooper. According to independent industry reports, the DevZone will allow partners to reference and utilize these new tools/resources significantly faster than existing products, enabling them to quickly evaluate, develop, and deploy Edge AI applications based on Ambarella's portfolios.

As it pertains to investors, this is critical as it enhances platform stickiness. Once an original equipment manufacturer (OEM) configures an image pipeline, creates a multi-stream workflow, verifies that these operations are functioning properly, and ultimately deploys those models on a specific silicon stack, switching suppliers becomes a costly and risky business, especially in the case of regulated and safety-critical applications.

Furthermore, in addition to this aspect of the DevZone, the DevZone provides further support for Ambarella's existing software story of providing developers with tools and the ability to map neural networks (created with various commonly used machine-learning frameworks such as Caffe, TensorFlow, and PyTorch) to Ambarella's processors without requiring any change to the model. With this capability, customers can easily transition from model development to actual production scale.

Ambarella operates in a fragmented landscape of edge AI, which includes both large platform manufacturers and lower-cost vision systems on chips. At the high end of the spectrum are competitors such as NVIDIA (NVDA), QUALCOMM (QCOM), and Mobileye (MBLY) that offer highly capable and software-rich platforms for the robotics and automotive perception markets. These high-end solutions tend to be built around general-purpose computing and, as such, are likely to be over-engineered, overly expensive, and too energy-intensive for always-on edge devices. At the low end of the spectrum, Ambarella competes against camera SoC vendors that focus on cost and serve primarily basic, single-camera designs but lose relevance as the marketplace moves toward multi-stream, multi-sensor, and AI-intensive workloads. This is the area where Ambarella is increasingly focusing its attention. In particular, Ambarella's newer CV-Series products are designed to provide higher AI capabilities while remaining within strict power and thermal restrictions. Strategically, Ambarella is not attempting to compete with "hyperscale" AI vendors solely on compute scale. Rather, Ambarella positions itself as a best-in-class supplier of edge perception systems, focusing on the combination of video image quality, low-power consumption, and on-device AI. As edge AI workloads increase dramatically across camera, fleet, robotic, and vehicle platforms, Ambarella has the ability to compete effectively, despite being outsized by larger competitors, particularly in markets where solutions no longer adequately meet customer needs.

Valuation Provides Upside Potential

From the perspective of relative valuation, Ambarella can be primarily compared with companies whose economic model is primarily vision-oriented edge perception, as opposed to lower-level compute or general-purpose compute. Here, Qualcomm is relevant to the analysis, especially when considering its automotive and IoT segments, which create overlap with Ambarella in the areas of advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS), vision processing, and edge AI inference.

Although neither Texas Instruments (TXN) nor NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) are considered direct competitors, both serve as a benchmark for mature automotive and industrial edge silicon products. Thus, including these two aids in determining what value Ambarella would receive if the company were valued as an industrial supplier and why Ambarella's AI-fuelled, vision-driven growth story deserves a greater-than-average premium valuation compared to the average discount faced by traditional industrial suppliers.

In contrast, NVIDIA, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Broadcom (AVGO) represent Hyperscale AI leaders with valuation multiples that are highly influenced by data center infrastructure and platform scale economics; therefore, a valuation comparison to these companies is not justified even if they compete directly with Ambarella in select edge use cases. Lastly, small-cap IoT and MCU companies such as Silicon Laboratories (SLAB) are not appropriate comparable companies as growth drivers, profit margin structures, and system roles are substantially different from Ambarella's vision-centric AI SoCs.

As of the time of this writing, Ambarella has a Price/Sales ratio of 7.8, which is notably higher compared to NXP Semiconductors at 5.0x, Mobileye at 4.5x, and Qualcomm at 3.9x. Although this may appear pricey at first, Ambarella has significant growth potential with consensus revenue estimates reflecting approximately 37% growth for the current fiscal year, while Mobileye, Qualcomm, and NXP's estimated revenue growth will be 4%, 2%, and 6%, respectively. Also, while Texas Instruments is carrying the highest multiple in this subgroup, TI should only realize a revenue growth estimate of 22.5% for the current fiscal year or more than intermittently below that of Ambarella's fair value growth rate projections. Texas Instruments also likely demands a higher valuation, since its operating margins currently stand at roughly 35%. Ambarella, on the other hand, still does not have positive operating margins as it continues to invest heavily, especially in R&D.

In 2022, the company's operating margins briefly reached a peak due to the ability to leverage existing legacy products and due to global supply-chain issues, which increased the price for AMBA's chips. Margins fell afterwards due to accelerated research and development spending on edge AI platforms during a revenue downturn associated with traditional product cycles. Now that edge AI revenue has become dominant and revenue is returning to growth levels, the company has started to resume operating leverage capabilities. I believe that as Ambarella continues to grow and scale, its operating margins will follow suit. This could result in a major expansion of its valuation multiples.

Further, institutional positioning for Ambarella increased over the last four consecutive quarters reported. The number of funds holding AMBA has grown from 296 in Q4 2024 to 325 in Q3 2025, while total shares held grew from 33,700,000 to 35,700,000. The trend indicates a rise in both the number of institutional interests in edge-AI and could provide additional upside momentum if this trend continued in Q4 2025. In terms of insider trading, there has been no meaningful change in 2025, as neither of the two largest shareholders from the executive team, Ex-CTO Les Kohn or CEO Fermi Wang, have significantly increased or decreased their share count.

Conclusion

In my opinion, the potential for significant growth opportunities in the areas of security AI, fleet video telematics, drones, and expansion into automotive sensors will provide a runway for future growth. While execution risk and competition will always exist within the Semiconductor industry, edge AI continues to be an area that will expand significantly in the foreseeable future.

Additionally, the new CV7 will allow Ambarella to enter the higher end of multi-stream, multi-sensor systems, which tend to have much higher unit prices and switching costs than typical single-camera applications. Lastly, the DevZone and software ecosystem will give Ambarella more potential to evolve beyond a chip supplier to original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and more into a company that creates a reusable platform for its customers that will help them enable themselves. Therefore, I see additional growth potential not just in AI data centers but also in the area of physical edge devices, where Ambarella can thrive.

data.title: KKR: The Value Case Is Strong, Despite The Macro Risks (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Private equity stocks have been in focus in recent months. The Tricolor and First Brands bankruptcies in late 2025 reminded investors that financial risk often lies just beneath the frothy equity market surface.

I downgraded shares of KKR & Co. (KKR) from a buy to a hold just about a year ago. Shares have declined 23% in that span, lagging the S&P 500 badly by almost 40 percentage points. Today, despite all the risks and unknowns in the private-asset arena, I am upgrading KKR to a buy.

I see the valuation as too compelling to remain neutral on, but the chart is nearing a critical juncture.

KKR Lags Sharply YoY

Back in November, KKR reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $1.41 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $1.30, while revenue of $5.5 billion, up 15% from the same period a year earlier, was not far from estimates. The Financials-sector firm tallied Fee-Related Earnings of $1.0 billion, a 3% YoY increase.

Shares rallied 1.7% in the session that followed, and that was well within the 6.3% expected post-earnings swing. Looking ahead to the February 5 Q4 report, the options market prices in a near-average 5.3% up or down move. The next dividend is slated for February 17, while implied volatility is a bit elevated at 37%. Short interest on shares of the $114 billion market cap company is low at 1.17%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, KKR's Total Operating Earnings rose 12% to $1.4 billion over the July through September period. Its Insurance Operating Earnings were $305 million, boosted by a $41 million positive impact from Global Atlantic's actuarial review. Total assets under management grew 16% to $723 billion, and Fee-Paying AUM also rose 16% to $585 billion.

KKR Color on Quarter

Operationally, KKR raised a near-record $43 billion in new capital (its second-highest quarterly figure) and $128 billion over the trailing 12 months. The company also invested a record $26 billion in Q3 and maintained $126 billion in dry powder. The management team detailed on the call several strategic deals from around mid-year, including acquiring a majority stake in HealthCare Royalty Partners, adding $3 billion to AUM, and a $2 billion strategic partnership with Japan Post Insurance for Global Atlantic.

The bottom-line beat was encouraging, but some of the EPS jump came from lower-quality sources, like transaction fees, fee-related performance revenues, and the one-time insurance item (Global Atlantic). KKR executives reaffirmed 2026 guidance for FRE per share of $4.50. The team was generally upbeat on the call but did warn of potential market volatility that could hinder current-year EPS.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to accelerate in 2026 with more solid gains in the out year. It comes after what is likely to have been a disappointing bottom-line increase in FY 2025. At the time of my January 2025 analysis, FY 2025 was expected to see $6.12 of non-GAAP EPS. Today, that number is just $5.00.

Still, with a high earnings growth rate, at least a market multiple is warranted, in my view. Given lighter EPS forecasts, the sellside has turned less upbeat in the last 90 days, as evidenced by a high 12 downgrades compared to just 3 upgrades. Still, profitability trends are healthy, earning shares an A- Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha's quantitative scoring system.

KKR: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts

On valuation, if we assume $6.75 of normalized forward operating EPS and apply a near-market 23x multiple, then shares should trade near $155. Now, a 20-35% EPS growth rate would normally point to a 30-40x P/E, but there are clear risks with the private equity and private credit market right now, so I discounted the multiple.

But there remains a significant margin of safety between the intrinsic value estimate and the current stock price.

Cheaper Today Compared to Last Year

Key risks for KKR include rising competition in the private-asset management industry, along with the chance of fee compression in the quarters and years ahead. Specifically, KKR's Asia II Fund was particularly weak in Q3, and that itself could weigh on profits.

What's more, the insurance strategy is unclear, as called out on the Q3 conference call.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Thursday, February 5 BMO. The company then presents at the UBS Financial Services Conference the following week.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares materially undervalued, in my opinion, KKR's technical situation is nearing a major test. Notice in the chart below that the stock remains in a symmetrical triangle consolidation pattern. This coil is close to the apex, and the presumption is that there will be a notable breakout or breakdown. Moreover, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart has ranged violently from oversold to overbought, providing little signal for trend followers.

The mixed risks are seen in the long-term 200-day moving average, too. Both that trend-indicator line and the short-term 50dma are virtually flat in their slopes, right near where KKR trades today. It doesn't end there-a high amount of volume by price is found between $110 and $130, making the current zone all the more pivotal.

Watch for the breakout or breakdown, potentially around the upcoming earnings date. For now, it's wait and see.

KKR: Shares Coiling, Volatile RSI

The Bottom Line

Despite the uncertain technical backdrop, I have a buy rating on KKR. I see shares of the asset management company as a compelling value, but there are macro risks.

data.title: Vishay Intertechnology: A Pick-And-Shovel Play For Cyclical Exposure
data.description:
The past year has been a challenging one for semiconductor companies without meaningful exposure to growth markets like AI or data centers, and as a diversified provider of “picks and shovels” components like MOSFETs, resistors, capacitors, and diodes, it has been slow going for Vishay Intertechnology (VSH).

These shares are basically flat since my last update on the company, though there’s been quite a bit of volatility over that time, including nearly 50% sell-offs and 50% rallies. That’s well below the performance of the broader semiconductor sector, as well as the industrial sector (arguably a better proxy given its end markets), and the stock has also lagged closer comparables like Diodes (DIOD), Infineon (IFNNY), ON Semiconductor (ON), and STMicro (STM), though not to quite the same extent.

Vishay management has chosen to make substantial investments into capacity even though this downturn, and while that is weighing on results today, it offers upside when markets recover. Vishay has good leverage to the “electrify everything” trend across industrial end markets, as well as increasing chip content in auto, aerospace, and defense markets, but this is more of a cyclical recovery story than a distinct catalyst-driven story.

More of the Same in Q4 Seems Likely

Due to report earnings on February 4, I don’t expect many changes from the results posted for the fourth quarter.

Revenue is likely to be basically flat on a sequential basis (but up around 10% on a year-over-year basis), and I would expect to see some sequential improvements in auto and industrial end markets given better-than-expected auto build volumes in the fourth quarter and some evidence of improving short-cycle industrial markets, not to mention ongoing spending on utility grid expansion and improvement. Aero/defense is probably not going to be a standout given delays in funding defense programs. AI/datacenter-related demand should be solid (servers and server power), but this is still a relatively small part of the business (around $30-$40 in content).

Gross margin is likely to remain depressed, as the company continues to deal with underutilized capacity and particularly the Newport facility, which has been a roughly 150bp drag on gross margin in recent quarters. This has been an issue for others like Renesas (RNECY) (RNECF), and there’s not much management can do other than to wait it out. Management has made the decision to run with excess capacity in the downturn in order to be better placed to meet stronger demand when the cycle eventually returns, and this strategy makes some sense when considering that the company lost share in the last upcycle because it didn’t have the available capacity to meet surging demand.

Operating margin will likewise remain depressed in the low single digits, and the company likely needs quarterly revenue back in the $850M - $900M range before double-digit operating margins are back in play.

2026 Should Be Better, but Not a V-Shaped Recovery

Guidance always matters, but I don’t expect particularly robust guidance for Vishay at this point in the cycle, as there isn’t a clear driver to reaccelerate the business back beyond the high single digits to low double digits.

The auto business is likely to see another year of relatively weak underlying builds, but I do see opportunities for growth in the coming year. New models have higher chip content, and Vishay is leveraged to increase ADAS content on these new models. There is likewise some movement toward increased hybrid production (which offers higher content for Vishay versus traditional powertrains), and Vishay should be in place to pick up some alternative sourcing as companies look to mitigate their reliance on Nexperia.

Industrial markets likewise offer some upside, but it seems premature to get too far ahead of the curve. Smart grid investments continue to drive demand for components like capacitors (revenue was up 20% in Q3’25), and I do see improving demand for automation and electrification.

Those two markets cover about 70% of quarterly revenue, but I do also expect some improvement in aero/defense and medical in 2026. Ongoing growth from AI and data center server and power demand seems probable as well, but again the magnitude is not nearly as transformative here as for companies supplying GPUs, TPUs, PAM4 DSPs, and so on.

The Outlook

Distributor demand has been stronger in the last two quarters, but channel inventories have stayed relatively flat, leading me to think that companies are slowly rebuilding inventories in anticipation of improving demand in 1H’26. Likewise, OEM demand perked up in the third quarter (up 6% sequentially), leading me to think that at the very least the inventory correction cycle is over now.

With excess capacity at Vishay and across the sector more broadly, I wouldn’t expect Vishay to meaningfully outperform underlying demand in 2026; the outperformance will appear if and when strong demand outstrips the excess capacity at Vishay’s rivals and the company’s decision to invest in additional capacity leaves it positioned to supply that incremental demand.

I’m expecting 8% revenue growth in FY’26 and double-digit growth in FY’27, leading to around 8% annualized growth over the next five years and long-term annualized growth closer to 6%-7%. Excess capacity will weigh on margins for a while, and I think double-digit operating margins will be tough to achieve until FY’27. I do expect the next up-cycle to take EBITDA margins back above 20%, but the long-term sustainable margin is likely more in the mid-to-high teens. Likewise, I don’t have that much confidence that Vishay can sustain free cash flow margins much beyond the mid-single-digits on a long-term basis.

Discounted cash flow suggests a potential long-term annualized return in the high single digits from here, and that’s not terrible, but it’s also not compelling. DCF is tricky with cyclical companies, though, and semiconductor stocks tend to trade more on their margins. Assuming that a return to double-digit operating margin in 2027 is a valid assumption, it supports a 6x multiple on FY’27 EBITDA; discounted back to today, that gets me to around $21 for a fair value target.

The Bottom Line

Is roughly 10% to 15% upside enough to justify owning Vishay? I think so, particularly given my view that the worst of the cycle is over and Vishay offers more leverage to recovering demand than many of its peers. It’s worth repeating that this is a cyclical recovery play—Vishay's products are “picks and shovels” components, and this isn’t a company that’s going to outperform on the basis of R&D-driven innovation. As a cyclical play, then, I see an argument for owning these shares, but prospective investors shouldn’t look at this as a potential long-term holding.

data.title: Wall Street Brunch: Megacaps And The Fed
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Apple and Microsoft lead a packed earnings week. (0:15) Fed decision looms as Powell faces White House investigation. (1:51) Winter storm grounds thousands of flights. (2:47)

The following is an abridged transcript:

Those shouts you hear are echoing from the Five Points to Wall Street, where we see who holds sway over the market. The megacap companies, born rightwise with the AI trade, or the Federal Reserve, and its monetary policy?

Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta Platforms (META) headline a heavy earnings calendar, alongside results from Tesla (TSLA), Boeing (BA), Visa (V), Mastercard (MA), Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX).

In the old rivalry of DOS vs. Mac, Microsoft’s report will be closely watched for signs of AI-driven Azure growth and margins, while Apple’s numbers should shed light on iPhone demand and its slower-moving AI strategy.

SA analyst KM Capital says the technical setup looks favorable, with Microsoft near oversold territory heading into earnings on Wednesday, while AI and cloud investments — backed up by the recent U.S. Air Force contract — continue to underpin growth.

Despite tariffs and geopolitical friction with China, Apple is still expected to deliver its largest year-over-year revenue jump in four years when it reports fiscal Q1 2026 results on Thursday.

HSBC analyst Nicolas Cote-Cool says Apple’s success is being driven largely by the iPhone 17 series — especially the Pro and Pro Max — as well as continued strength for the iPhone 16 in key emerging markets.

Also on the earnings calendar:

  • Tuesday we hear from Boeing (BA), UnitedHealth (UNH), Texas Instruments (TXN), RTX (RTX) and General Motors (GM).
  • On Wednesday, Microsoft and Meta are joined by Tesla (TSLA), IBM (IBM) and AT&T (T).
  • Thursday brings payments and heavy machinery with Visa (V), Mastercard (MA) and Caterpillar (CAT).
  • And we wrap up on Friday with Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), American Express (AXP) and Verizon (VZ).

Turning our attention to the Fed, the FOMC rate decision comes Wednesday, but with the market pricing in a 99% chance rates are on hold, never mind all that.

The question is what Chairman Jerome Powell will say about the criminal investigation launched by the White House into him and the Fed about the renovation of the central bank’s HQ.

The easy answer is that he’ll say he can’t comment on an ongoing investigation, but he already had – coming out with one of the boldest challenges of any part of the government to President Trump when he tied the subpoenas directly to Trump’s desire for lower rates.

Seeking Alpha analysts have emphasized that the investigation represents an unprecedented escalation since the Fed's creation in 1913.

This past week Powell got some perceived backing from the Supreme Court, as justices expressed unease over Trump's attempt to oust Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook.

In Davos, Trump said he had no plans to fire Powell, but also said the Fed chief would not be happy if he stays on as a governor when his term as chairman expires.

In the news this weekend, the powerful winter storm disrupted air travel across much of the United States, triggering thousands of flight cancellations at major airports.

The storm brought snow, ice and freezing rain across a wide swath of the country, putting nearly 180M people under weather alerts. Forecasters say the system will move into the Northeast after hitting the South, with heavy snowfall expected from Washington to Boston.

Trump threatened to implement 100% tariffs on goods imported from Canada if the country goes ahead with a newly signed trade agreement with China.

He posted “If Canada makes a deal with China, it will immediately be hit with a 100% tariff against all Canadian goods and products coming into the U.S.A.”

And American workers have embraced artificial intelligence at a remarkable pace, according to a new Gallup poll showing AI tools are now embedded in daily routines across multiple industries.

A survey of more than 22,000 U.S. workers found that 12% of employed adults now use AI daily in their jobs, with about a quarter using it a few times a week. Nearly half report using AI at least a few times a year — a big jump from 2023, when just 21% used AI even occasionally.

That last segment was edited by my beloved AI assistant.

And for income investors, Clorox (CLX) and Paychex (PAYX) go ex-dividend on Wednesday.

Clorox pays out on Feb. 13, and Paychex pays out on Feb. 27.

On Friday, Costco (COST) and Morgan Stanley (MS) go ex-dividend, with both paying out on Feb. 13.

data.description:
Investment Philosophy:

Purchase durable business franchises that are selling at a discount to their intrinsic value.

Investment Process:

We utilize a business owner’s approach to investing, thoroughly investigating the economics of the business and the quality of the management team. Some of the characteristics of good businesses include strong recurring revenue and attractive returns-on-invested capital (ROIC). We have a strong orientation to low absolute and relative valuation, which are key to the execution of our investment strategy. A new idea will come from a variety of sources including company visits, screens, conferences, trade periodicals and general reading. All members of the research team are responsible for fundamental research.

Once an investment opportunity is identified it is put through an extensive due diligence process, which typically includes management interviews and site visits. When an acceptable level of conviction is achieved, the appropriate weighting (considering liquidity, valuation, etc.) is discussed and determined. A new company purchased in the portfolio will usually have an initial position size of 2-4%. The portfolio generally consists of 25-40 companies and is diversified across industries.

We are long-term investors, a typical holding period for our companies is three to five years, and portfolio turnover averages 20-40% per annum.

data.title: Carillon Chartwell Small Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The summer's short squeeze, which boosted low-quality stocks, continued in the fourth quarter. The UBS High Short Interest Index advanced more than 25% during the first two weeks while major stock market averages barely budged. During this period, talk of a bubble grew louder and eroded the confidence of investors who had bet on high-flying shares of unprofitable companies with unproven business models. As their collective anxiety intensified, the speculative fever that had gripped the market for much of the previous six months began to break.

In the ensuing weeks, a sense of market normalcy slowly emerged. Many investors started questioning narratives that had fueled the mania, shifting their focus from making a quick buck or keeping pace with benchmarks to considering business durability, financial strength, and profitability. The promise of artificial intelligence (Al) remained, and so did expectations for significant investment in Al infrastructure. But investors began to scrutinize the funding for those investments and the future revenue and earnings available to companies in the Al ecosystem. Their gaze also may have broadened a bit, allowing them to see a larger set of attractive industries and companies that had been obscured by their excitement about Al.

Value stocks benefited from this intra-quarter market rotation. After lagging in October, they improved in November and December to end the quarter ahead of growth stocks in all market-cap segments. Total return for the Russell 2000 Value® Index, the Carillon Chartwell Small Cap Value Fund's benchmark, was 3.3% in the fourth quarter. The best-performing sectors in the index were healthcare, materials, and communication services. The worst-performing sectors were information technology, consumer staples, and consumer discretionary.

American Woodmark manufactures kitchen and bath cabinetry for remodeling and new residential construction. The company's share price weakened amid slowing housing activity and higher input costs, which weighed on margins and tempered near-term expectations.

Kemper is a property and casualty insurer focusing on non-standard personal lines of coverage distributed through independent agents. The company's stock lagged due to concerns about intensifying auto insurance competition and sustained pressure from higher claim severity.

Visteon is a leading supplier of automotive cockpit electronics for original equipment manufacturers. The company's share price lagged as customers' production disruptions amplified broader industry softness.

Outlook

Low-quality leadership in 2025, especially during the short squeeze of late summer and early fall, was a significant challenge that negatively impacted the Fund’s relative performance. Historically, such headwinds have dissipated after six to nine months. This latest episode began in April, so the timing of the fourth-quarter rotation is encouraging; we’re optimistic that market conditions are poised to continue to normalize.

We remained committed to our philosophy and process throughout the past year, continuing to make investment decisions that prioritized value, quality, and the potential for fundamental improvement. We were also more active than usual, aiming to take advantage of macroeconomic-driven volatility and the extreme performance bifurcation in the small-cap universe driven by narrow market leadership.

We believe these opportunistic actions enhanced the Fund’s portfolio and that many of its holdings, particularly the newest additions, appear to be attractively valued. Our team will continue diligently pursuing promising investment opportunities for the Fund while working to prudently maintain a well-balanced portfolio. The Fund’s current positioning reflects this bottom-up process with modest overweights in the industrials, consumer staples, and materials sectors and modest underweights in the financials, communication services, and consumer discretionary sectors.

Risk Considerations:

Mutual fund investing involves risk, including the potential loss of principal. Small Cap stocks may be less liquid and are more susceptible to market volatility because they may not have the management experience, financial resources, product diversification, or competitive strengths and tend to be sold less often and in smaller amounts relative to larger companies. Foreign securities involve greater currency valuation, economic, political, and regulatory environment risk relative to US securities. The Fund may invest in ETFs and therefore would be subject to the same risks as the underlying securities in which the ETF invests as well as entails higher expenses than if invested into the underlying ETF directly.

Definitions

Artificial intelligence (AI) – Technology that enables computers to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity and autonomy.

Growth investing – a stock-buying strategy that focuses on companies expected to grow at an above-average rate compared to their industry or the market.

Guidance – Statements from managers of publicly traded companies about their expectations for near-term profits or losses.

Headwind – An event or market force that hinders the prospects for performance in an individual investment or group of investments.

Market capitalization, or market cap – The total dollar market value of a company’s outstanding shares of stock.

Narrow – Describes the relationship between the median and the mean of a market index: When a few data outliers result in a mean that is substantially larger (or smaller) than the median of the full data set, then the performance of the entire index is being driven by a “narrow” selection of companies. An index supported by “broad” market movements is one where the median is closer to the mean.

Original equipment manufacturers (OEM) – Make parts for use in other firms’ finished products.

Overweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Positioning – Assessments of whether professional investors are, on the whole, bullish or bearish on a particular security, industry, sector, market capitalization or other area of the market, as reflected by the extent to which they are invested in the area of the market in question.

Quality investing – Investment strategies that seek to invest in companies with low debt, stable earnings, consistent asset growth, and strong

corporate governance, as reflected in metrics such return to equity and debt to equity ratios, as well as to earnings variability.

Relative performance – Performance calculated with similar and comparable assets to determine the value of the assets relative to each other.

Rotation – the movement of investments in securities from one industry, sector, factor, or asset class to another as market participants react to or try to anticipate the next stage of the economic cycle.

Short squeeze – When the price of an asset rises sharply, forcing short sellers to buy it to avoid incurring excessive losses. Those purchases put more upward pressure on the asset price.

Total return – The actual rate of return of an investment or investments over a given period of time. Total return includes interest, capital gains, dividends, and distributions realized over the specified period.

Underweight – A portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Value investing – Investment strategies that seek to invest in stocks that appear to be trading for less than their intrinsic or book value.

Indices

The Russell 2000® Value Index, the Fund’s benchmark index, is an unmanaged index that measures the performance of those Russell 2000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values.

The UBS High Short Interest Index tracks a basket of 100 stocks with the highest short utilization rate, screened for a market capitalization of less than $1 billion.

data.title: Davenport Equity Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Q4 2025 Market Review

The Davenport Equity Opportunities Fund (DEOPX) declined 4.07% during the fourth quarter, finishing the year down 2.61%. This compared to the Russell Mid Cap® Index, which experienced gains of 0.16% and 10.60% for the quarter and year, respectively. This was certainly a difficult close to a frustrating year. While we had several standout performers throughout the year, their returns were muted by a handful of key detractors. This made it difficult to keep up with the index, which received additional thrust from AI momentum darlings that continued their ascent in spite of stratospheric valuations. While frustrated, we have tremendous faith in our process and continue to execute against it with rigorous discipline. We expect this commitment to quality, cash flow, capital allocation and valuation to pay off over time.

Fund Update

Contributors: Align Technology Inc. (ALGN) was the strategy’s top performer during the period, bouncing nearly 25% from depressed levels alongside stabilizing results and more constructive price commentary. Other top contributors included Markel Corp (MKL), Etsy Inc. (ETSY) and new addition Cooper Companies Inc. (COO). We elected to exit our position in Etsy with the stock on a spike. We provide more detail on our purchase of Cooper below.

Detractors: Key detractors included Alexandra Real Estate Equities Inc. (ARE), Mobileye Global Inc. (MBLY), Generac Holdings Inc. (GNRC) and Watsco Inc. (WSO). In the case of ARE, we had begun to build a position into the company’s most recent earnings report; however, abandoned the effort following deterioration in business trends which resulted in a dramatic change in the company’s cash flow outlook. On the other hand, we added to positions in Watsco and Generac amid weakness.

Fund Activity

We initiated a position in Cooper Companies during the quarter. Cooper is the world’s second-largest player in the contact lens market, just behind Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and slightly ahead of Alcon AG (ALC). We have long admired the company’s annuity-like business model, as contact lens wearers require regular replacements and category growth is also supported by the natural decline in visual acuity with age. We further respect Cooper’s reputation as an R&D leader, underscored by its pioneering products, including the only FDA-approved pediatric contact lens for myopia management and a joint venture for pediatric spectacle lenses. Specialty lenses for astigmatism and multi-focal needs, along with a shift toward daily disposable lenses, should continue to support premium pricing and strong revenue growth. With the stock experiencing a significant decline following a disappointing quarterly earnings report, we were able to initiate a position with the shares cheaper than they have been in over a decade. After our purchase, we were pleased to see several meaningful insider purchases alongside the announcement of a strategic review process that could unlock value from the sale on non-core operations.

We also initiated a position in Carrier Global Corp (CARR), largest provider of heating, ventilation and air- conditioning (HVAC) equipment and services for the US residential market. Long term, we note the company is well positioned to benefit from secular growth drivers such as decarbonization, energy efficiency mandates, and heat pump adoption. Further, the company’s expanding services and aftermarket business provides a significant runway for margin improvement. Despite short-term headwinds in US residential HVAC, Carrier’s commercial and international segments are seeing healthy growth, and the company is also well positioned to benefit from macro catalysts like a housing recovery and increased infrastructure spending. With the stock trading at a significant discount to peers and historical averages, we thought the shares timely. Finally, we note that we are in good company with this belief given that CEO Dave Gitlin also purchased ~$1.0 million of stock at similar levels.

Conclusion

In sum, we are thankful for the opportunity to leave a disappointing year in the rearview mirror and remain enthusiastic about the strategy’s positioning headed into 2026. Though we made some mistakes and found ourselves on the wrong side of the momentum trade in 2025, we continued to apply our disciplined approach to emphasizing quality and optimizing around risk/reward. We are excited about the new businesses we are able to add to the fold during the year and feel the strategy is differentiated and timely at current levels.

Sincerely,

George L. Smith III, CFA®

Christopher G. Pearson, CFA®

data.title: Dodge & Cox Balanced Fund Q4 2025 Shareholder Letter
data.description:
Market Commentary

Financial markets delivered another strong year in 2025, though not without turbulence from tariff uncertainty, a record 43-day government shutdown, and a cooling labor market. The S&P 500 Index rose 18%, notching its third consecutive year of double-digit gains. Artificial intelligence remained a dominant theme, and Information Technology and Communication Services were the top-contributing sectors. Market concentration stayed elevated, and the ten largest companies contributed more than half of the Index’s gains. Overseas, international equities beat U.S. stocks by roughly 15 percentage points—a historically wide margin—and emerging markets had their strongest showing in nearly a decade.

Bonds had a solid year as well. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index returned over 7%, marking its best year since 2020, as interest rates declined and corporate and mortgage-backed bonds outperformed.

The U.S. economy remained resilient with third-quarter GDP growing at a brisk 4.3% annualized rate. But cracks emerged: the unemployment rate climbed to a four-year high of 4.6% by November. As inflation continued to moderate, the Federal Reserve responded with three rate cuts throughout the year, including two in the fourth quarter, bringing cumulative easing to 175 basis points since September 2024. With rates now closer to neutral, Fed Chair Powell signaled the Fed can be patient before making its next move.

Current month-end performance can be obtained at dodgeandcox.com or by calling 800-621-3979.

Portfolio Strategy

Broad U.S. equity valuations look stretched by historical standards, suggesting more modest future returns. In fixed income, real yields are attractive, but tight credit spreads call for discipline. Against this backdrop, the Fund’s asset allocation remained relatively steady, but this stability masks meaningful activity at the security level. As of December 31, the Fund held 48% in U.S. equities, 18% in non-U.S. equities, and 34% in fixed income securities plus cash.2

In 2025, we initiated 13 new equity positions in companies where we believe long-term fundamentals are not reflected in current prices. New holdings span semiconductor equipment, insurance brokers, food products, and software. We also built a position in the Dodge & Cox Emerging Markets Stock Fund, which provides efficient exposure to a diverse group of emerging market companies trading at appealing valuations.3 At the same time, we trimmed or exited positions where valuations became less compelling. As a result, we believe the equity portfolio is more reasonably priced and diversified than the broader market.

Within fixed income, we shifted the mix—reducing the portfolio's Credit sector exposure (now near multi-year lows) and adding to Securitized holdings (near multi-year highs). With credit spreads near their tightest levels in decades, we favor high-quality securitized assets over chasing yield in expensive corporate credit. The Fund’s Securitized allocation centers on Agency4 mortgage-backed securities, which offer solid return potential across rate environments and high liquidity.

Outlook

We’re optimistic about the Fund’s long-term return prospects. The combination of attractively valued equities and a diversified bond portfolio positions the Fund well for a range of outcomes—and we're prepared to act when volatility creates opportunity. Thank you for your continued confidence in Dodge & Cox.

Performance Review (Fund’s Class I Shares) Fourth Quarter

Equity security selection drove the Fund’s relative underperformance. This was partially offset by positive equity sector allocation.

Equity Portfolio (vs. S&P 500)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Industrials stock selection, including FedEx (FDX);
  • Stock selection and underweight position in Information Technology, particularly underweight position in Microsoft (MSFT);
  • Overweight position in Health Care, primarily Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) and GSK (GSK); and
  • Underweight position in Meta Platforms (META).

Key detractors from relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Financials stock selection, mainly Fiserv (FISV);
  • Energy holdings, notably Occidental Petroleum (OXY);
  • Communication Services stock selection, including Charter Communications (CHTR) and underweight position in Alphabet (GOOG); and holding in Alibaba (BABA).

Fixed Income Portfolio (vs. Bloomberg U.S. Agg)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Security selection within Agency MBS and overweight to the sector;
  • Underweight to U.S. Treasuries; and
  • Credit security selection, notably British American Tobacco and Oracle.

Key detractors from relative results included the portfolio’s:

  • Position in Charter Communications.

2025

Strong security selection within equities was the main driver of the Fund’s relative outperformance. Fixed income security selection and sector allocation also benefited relative results. These factors were partially offset by weak equity sector allocation.

Equity Portfolio (vs. S&P 500)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Consumer Discretionary stock selection and underweight position;
  • Health Care stock selection, mainly CVS Health (CVS) and GSK;
  • Industrials holdings, notably RTX (RTX);
  • Consumer Staples stock selection and underweight position; and
  • Holding in Banco Santander (SAN).

Key detractors from relative results included the portfolio’s:

  • Underweight position in Information Technology;
  • Materials stock selection and overweight position;
  • Communication Services holdings and underweight position, particularly Charter Communications; and
  • Positions in Fiserv, Occidental Petroleum, and Avantor (AVTR).

  • Security selection within Agency MBS and overweight to the sector; and
  • Underweight to U.S. Treasuries and overweight to corporate bonds.
